title: EITT Boundary Condition and Discovery Roadmap (Concise JSON for Claude)
author: Copilot (for Claude coworker)
purpose: Make the temporal-autocorrelation boundary explicit, state the open mathematical problem clearly, and provide a concrete, reproducible discovery plan (empirical, simulation, analytic) suitable for inclusion in a presentation or handed to collaborators.

clear_boundary_condition.plain_statement: EITT (empirical near-invariance of Shannon entropy under geometric-mean temporal decimation) is observed only when the compositional time series exhibits nontrivial temporal persistence at the decimation scale. Without temporal persistence the effect disappears or is not reliably upward.

operational_tests.name: CLR lag-1 ACF test
operational_tests.description: Compute CLR transform of the composition, compute per-component lag-1 autocorrelations, and report the mean and distribution. Require mean(lag1_acf) > tau_1 (suggested tau_1 = 0.1) as a minimal persistence indicator.

operational_tests.name: Block variance reduction test
operational_tests.description: For block size M, compute nonoverlapping block means and their covariance Cov_emp. Compare to iid null by permuting time order or resampling; compute block_variance_ratio = Var_iid(block_means) / Var_emp(block_means). Require ratio > R (suggested R = 1.5) to indicate meaningful persistence.

operational_tests.name: Direction consistency test
operational_tests.description: Compute pct_change_H across many independent windows; require sign consistency (majority upward) and nonzero mean with bootstrap CI not crossing zero.

open_mathematical_problem.concise_statement: Derive a closed-form expression or tight asymptotic bound for the expected entropy change delta_H under M-block geometric decimation for a stationary compositional stochastic process, expressed in terms of the process's autocovariance structure and the Frechet/CLR center.
open_mathematical_problem.target_formula_template: delta_H(M) = E[H(geom_mean_M(X))] - H(x_bar) = LeadingTerm(Sigma(h), M, x_star) + Remainder(Sigma(h), M), with LeadingTerm = (1/2) tr(|Hess_H(x_star)| * Cov(x_bar_M)) under mild regularity.
desired_properties_of_solution: Explicit dependence on lag-h covariances Sigma(h) or an effective autocorrelation scalar rho_eff.
Asymptotic correctness as M -> infinity and small-Cov expansion validity conditions.
A bound on the remainder term with clear assumptions (mixing rates, moment bounds).
open_mathematical_problem.why_this_matters: A closed form or tight bound would (a) explain the small magnitude of observed delta_H, (b) convert the qualitative boundary into quantitative thresholds, and (c) connect EITT to information-geometric or spectral process properties.

conjecture_1.text: Second-order Hessian approximation suffices: for stationary, weakly dependent compositional processes with bounded moments, the leading term of entropy change under geometric decimation is delta_H = (1/2) tr( |Hess_H(x_star)| * Cov(x_bar_M) ) + o(||Cov||).
conjecture_1.implication: If true, one can predict delta_H from estimated Cov(x_bar_M) and x_star without running decimation.

conjecture_2.text: There exists a threshold function f(M, rho_eff) such that if rho_eff >= f(M) then EITT holds within tolerance epsilon (|delta_H| <= epsilon).
conjecture_2.implication: This would convert the qualitative boundary condition into a quantitative threshold for autocorrelation required at each M.

conjecture_3.text: Information-geometric interpretation: delta_H can be expressed as a quadratic form in the tangent space at x_star under the Fisher-Rao metric (or an Aitchison-compatible metric), linking Hessian footprint to curvature of the simplex.
conjecture_3.implication: A geometric derivation could produce tighter, interpretable bounds and connect to rate-distortion or optimal compression.

discovery_direction_and_plan.overview: Three parallel tracks: empirical mapping, controlled simulation, and analytic derivation. Each track produces artifacts that cross-validate the others.

empirical_track.goal: Map where EITT holds across real datasets and quantify predictive power of the Hessian approximation.
steps: For each dataset and M in {1,7,30,91,365}: compute CLR, estimate x_star, compute Cov(x_bar_M), compute Hessian prediction delta_H_hess = 0.5 * tr(|Hess_H(x_star)| * Cov(x_bar_M)), compute observed delta_H and bootstrap CI, compute diagnostics (lag1_acf_mean, block_variance_ratio).
Produce a per-dataset table: {dataset, M, lag1_acf_mean, block_variance_ratio, observed delta_H (pct, CI), predicted delta_H_hess (pct), bootstrap percentiles, EITT_flag}.
Flag datasets where Hessian prediction explains > 80% of observed delta_H.

simulation_track.goal: Control process parameters to map delta_H as a function of autocorrelation and noise scale; validate analytic approximations.

designs.name: CLR-Gaussian VAR(1)
designs.spec: Simulate Z_t in CLR space as VAR(1), vary spectral radius rho in [0,0.99], vary innovation covariance scale, map back to simplex via inverse CLR, compute delta_H(M).

designs.name: Dirichlet autoregressive
designs.spec: Simulate compositional autoregressive processes to test non-Gaussian behavior.

designs.name: IID null and permutation
designs.spec: Resample marginals iid and confirm delta_H approximately 0 or no consistent upward drift.

analytic_track.goal: Prove the leading term and bound the remainder under clear assumptions.
first_steps: Write Taylor expansion of H around x_star for geometric mean of M samples; express geometric mean in terms of log-space averages and propagate via multivariate Taylor expansion.
Express Cov(x_bar_M) in terms of Sigma(h): Cov(x_bar_M) = (1/M^2) sum_{i,j=0}^{M-1} Sigma(i-j) and substitute into the Hessian term.
Use mixing conditions or spectral density to bound remainder terms; derive conditions under which remainder = o(||Cov||).
mathematical_tools: Matrix calculus for Hessian and trace manipulations
Lyapunov sums for linear VAR covariance aggregation
Spectral density / Wiener-Khinchin theorem for stationary processes
concrete_first_proofs_to_try: Prove that for a CLR-Gaussian VAR(1) with spectral radius rho<1 and small innovation covariance, the leading term of E[H(geom_mean)] - H(x_bar) equals 0.5 tr(|Hess_H(x_star)| * Cov(x_bar_M)) + o(||Cov||).
Show that Cov(x_bar_M) = (1/M^2) sum_{h=-(M-1)}^{M-1} (M-|h|) Sigma(h) and substitute into the Hessian formula to get an explicit dependence on Sigma(h).

slide_1.title: EITT - Statement and Boundary
bullets: Empirical near-invariance: Shannon entropy changes very little under geometric-mean decimation for autocorrelated compositional time series.
Boundary condition: requires temporal persistence at the decimation scale (diagnostic thresholds provided).

slide_2.title: Mechanism (Hessian Footprint)
bullets: Geometric mean reduces variance; entropy is concave.
This explains systematic upward drift and its small magnitude.

slide_3.title: Open Problem
bullets: Derive closed form or tight bound for delta_H(M) in terms of Sigma(h) and x_star.
Desired: asymptotic correctness, computability from estimated covariances, and explicit remainder bounds.

slide_4.title: Discovery Roadmap (Actionable)
bullets: Empirical sweep across datasets (produce master CSV).
Simulation grid (rho, M, noise) and plots delta_H vs rho.
Analytic derivation of leading term and remainder bound.
Deliverables: master CSV, simulation plots, technical note.

slide_5.title: Invitation
bullets: We provide data and scripts. Can you derive the closed form or tighten the bound?
Contact: repository and scripts included in the package.

quick_wins: Run empirical sweep and produce master CSV and a one-page summary.
Run CLR VAR(1) simulation for a small rho grid and produce delta_H vs rho plots for M=365 and M=30.
